1: A theme: The book opens with the prophet's name meaning 'YHWH remembers' — the program of the entire book. Christ is the ultimate expression of YHWH's remembering his covenant: 'God, having at many times and in various ways spoken to our fathers by the prophets, has in these last days spoken to us by his Son' (Hebrews 1:1-2). Zechariah's eight night visions are YHWH's detailed covenant-remembrance delivered through the prophet; the NT's fulfillment is the same covenant-remembrance embodied in the person of the Son.
2: A theme: YHWH's great anger at the fathers is the basis for the restoration call. Christ is the one who takes this covenant wrath onto himself: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The anger YHWH felt toward the fathers — which produced the exile — is the same anger that was exhausted at the cross. The restoration Zechariah announces is grounded in the same atonement.
3: A theme: 'Return to me and I will return to you, declares the LORD of hosts.' The mutual-return formula is the OT's most concentrated statement of covenant reconciliation. Christ is the embodiment of YHWH's return to his people: the incarnation is YHWH coming back to dwell with his people after the exile's separation. The NT form: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28) — Christ's invitation is the incarnate form of YHWH's return-call.
4: A theme: The call to not be like the fathers who refused the prophets' message is a call to receive the final Prophet. The 'former prophets' who were rejected find their ultimate fulfillment in the one who is greater than all the prophets: 'Something greater than the temple is here' (Matthew 12:6); 'Something greater than Solomon is here' (Matthew 12:42). Christ is the prophet whose word the fathers refused, the shepherd who was rejected (Zechariah 11:8-13; Matthew 21:33-39).
5: A theme: 'Your fathers, where are they? And the prophets, do they live forever?' The transience of generations contrasts with the permanence of YHWH's word. Christ is the permanent Word: 'Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away' (Matthew 24:35). The prophets who spoke YHWH's word died, but the Word himself is eternal (John 1:1-2): 'Before Abraham was, I am' (John 8:58). The prophet's question points beyond itself to the one who is not merely a vehicle of the word but is the Word.
6: A theme: YHWH's words did overtake the fathers in judgment — the exile proved the word true. Christ is the one in whom all prophetic words find their 'yes and amen' (2 Corinthians 1:20): 'all the promises of God find their Yes in him.' The fathers learned too late that YHWH's word stands; those who receive Christ receive the one who is himself the standing, faithful word of God (Revelation 19:13: his name is called the Word of God).
7: A theme: The dated formula (second year of Darius) grounds the visions in history. Christ enters history at a precise moment: 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law' (Galatians 4:4). The Incarnation is the ultimate night-vision — the divine presence appearing in history with a specific date, specific location, specific human context.
8: A direct revelation: The man on the red horse standing among the myrtle trees in the glen is identified in v11 as the Angel of the LORD — the pre-incarnate Christ. The theophanic figure who patrols the earth and intercedes for Israel (v12) is the divine-human person whose ultimate patrol is the incarnation (John 1:14: he came to his own). The myrtle trees — associated with restoration (Isaiah 41:19; Zechariah 55:13) — frame the messianic figure in the colors of covenant-blessing. The red horse anticipates the warrior-Messiah of Revelation 6:4 and Zechariah 19:11.
9: A direct revelation: The interpretive angel explains the first vision to Zechariah, mediating divine revelation. Christ is the ultimate mediator of revelation: 'No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known' (John 1:18). The angel who speaks in Zechariah is a type of the incarnate Son who speaks the Father's words directly: 'I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak' (John 12:49).
10: A theme: The divine patrol riders report that all the earth is at rest — the Pax Romana will be the historical context of Christ's first coming. Luke 2:1 begins the birth narrative with Caesar Augustus's empire-wide census — the world at rest under Roman peace. Christ was born into exactly the world Zechariah's first vision described: the nations at ease while God's people waited for restoration. The patrols' report is the historical preparation for the incarnation.
11: A theme: The divine patrol confirms the earth's rest — the problem that makes restoration necessary. The world's prosperity while Jerusalem suffers is the condition Christ enters. He comes not when the world is already perfect but into the midst of its complacency: 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). The quiet earth and the suffering city is the setting for the divine intervention that is the first coming.
12: A direct revelation: The Angel of the LORD's intercession — 'O LORD of hosts, how long?' — is the pre-incarnate Christ interceding for Israel. The heavenly intercessor who pleads for restoration in Zechariah's vision is the same one who, incarnate, 'always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). The lament of the divine intercessor ('how long have you had no mercy on Jerusalem?') finds its ultimate expression in Christ's lament: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, how often would I have gathered your children together' (Matthew 23:37).
13: A direct revelation: YHWH answers the Angel of the LORD's intercession with 'gracious and comforting words.' The intercessor's prayer is heard; the Father responds to the Son's intercession with covenant comfort. The NT parallel: 'Therefore he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). The gracious words YHWH speaks to the Angel become the restoration promises; Christ's intercession obtains the gracious words spoken over his people.
14: A direct revelation: YHWH's great zeal for Jerusalem — the passionate, jealous divine love that drives the restoration — is the character of Christ toward his people. The Gospel of John presents Jesus as animated by the same passion: the temple-cleansing (John 2:17: zeal for your house will consume me, citing Psalms 69:9); the weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41); the persistent love for the city that rejected him (Matthew 23:37). YHWH's qinah for Zion is Christ's consuming love for his people.
15: A theme: YHWH's great anger at the nations that exceeded their divine mandate in punishing Israel foreshadows the judgment on all who harm Christ's body. 'Whoever touches you touches the apple of his eye' (Zechariah 2:8). Paul: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?' (Acts 9:4) — Christ identifies himself with the persecuted church. The nations that exceeded their mandate in punishing Israel are a type of all who oppose Christ's people, and their judgment is the judgment on those who touch the apple of God's eye.
16: A direct revelation: 'I have returned to Jerusalem with mercy; my house shall be built in it.' The return of YHWH to Jerusalem with mercy is the incarnation: Christ, 'full of grace and truth' (John 1:14), is YHWH's return to his people. The measuring line stretched over Jerusalem for rebuilding is Christ's declaration about building a new temple: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up' (John 2:19). The house being built is Christ's body, the church, built on the cornerstone (Ephesians 2:20-22).
17: A theme: The triple 'again' of YHWH's renewed blessing — again prosperity, again comfort, again choosing Jerusalem — finds its ultimate expression in Christ's renewed covenant. 'Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God' (Revelation 21:3). The three 'agains' of Zechariah's restoration oracle become the permanent 'forever' of the new creation.
18: A type: The four horns that scattered Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem represent the world-powers arrayed against God's people throughout history. Christ is the one who defeats the ultimate four-directional opposition: at the cross, he defeated sin, death, the devil, and the powers (Colossians 2:15: he disarmed the rulers and authorities). The four craftsmen who overthrow the four horns are a type of Christ's definitive defeat of all world-powers that oppose the covenant community.
19: A type: The scattering of Judah, Israel, and Jerusalem by the four horn-powers is the background condition that Christ reverses by gathering his scattered people: 'I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will listen to my voice. So there will be one flock, one shepherd' (John 10:16). The scattered flock is gathered by the Good Shepherd who gives his life for the sheep.
20: A type: The four craftsmen who come to terrify and cast down the scattering horn-powers are a type of Christ's redemptive work. At the cross, Christ confronted every power arrayed against the covenant community: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The craftsmen who overthrow the horns one by one are the historical shadows of Christ's definitive disarming of the powers.
21: A type: The purpose of the four craftsmen — to terrify and cast down the scattering nations — is the ultimate purpose of Christ's victory at the cross. 'Now is the judgment of this world; now will the ruler of this world be cast out' (John 12:31). The world-powers that scattered Israel are cast down; the ruler of this world who scattered humanity from God is cast down in the definitive act of the cross and resurrection.
